message:
chore: sync incremental multi-agent changes

Minor updates from ongoing collaborative development:
- action_timeline.rs: Screen refinement
- text_effects.rs: Effect tweak
- input_forwarding.rs: Input handling improvement
- program.rs: Runtime adjustment
- beads sync

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"bd-2ueu.1","title":"PTY Spawn and Management","description":"Spawn and manage pseudo-terminal for child process.

## Requirements
- Fork with PTY allocation
- Shell spawning (configurable)
- Environment variable passing
- Clean process termination

## Implementation
- Build on ftui-pty crate
- PtyProcess struct
- spawn(), kill(), is_alive()

## Tests
- Shell spawn success
- Environment inheritance
- Clean termination

## Acceptance Criteria
- Deliver the behavior implied by the title: PTY Spawn and Management
- Alien Artifact: define invariants + failure modes; if heuristics exist, specify an explainable decision rule (evidence ledger / Bayes factors where applicable)
- Extreme Optimization: baseline -> profile -> opportunity matrix; for each perf change include an isomorphism proof + golden output checksums
- Unit tests: happy path + edge cases + regression fixtures; add property tests for core invariants where applicable
- E2E: scripts with verbose JSONL logging (env, capabilities, timings, seed, checksums) + deterministic mode

 {"id":"bd-2ueu.2","title":"ANSI Parser Integration","description":"Parse ANSI escape sequences from PTY output.

## Requirements
- CSI sequences (cursor, color, erase)
- OSC sequences (window title, clipboard)
- DCS sequences (sixel stub for future)
- UTF-8 text handling with grapheme awareness

## Proposed API

```rust
pub trait AnsiHandler {
    fn print(&mut self, c: char);
    fn execute(&mut self, byte: u8);  // C0 controls
    fn csi_dispatch(&mut self, params: &[i64], intermediates: &[u8], c: char);
    fn osc_dispatch(&mut self, params: &[&str]);
    fn esc_dispatch(&mut self, intermediates: &[u8], c: char);
}

pub struct AnsiParser {
    state: ParserState,
    params: Vec<i64>,
    intermediates: Vec<u8>,
    osc_data: String,
}

impl AnsiParser {
    pub fn new() -> Self;
    
    /// Parse bytes and dispatch to handler
    pub fn parse<H: AnsiHandler>(&mut self, data: &[u8], handler: &mut H);
    
    /// Reset parser state
    pub fn reset(&mut self);
}

// State machine states (simplified)
enum ParserState {
    Ground,
    Escape,
    CsiEntry,
    CsiParam,
    OscString,
    // ... more states
}
```

## Implementation Strategy

### Use vte crate
The vte crate provides a well-tested ANSI parser. Wrap it with our AnsiHandler trait.

```rust
use vte::{Parser, Perform};

struct VteAdapter<H: AnsiHandler> {
    handler: H,
}

impl<H: AnsiHandler> Perform for VteAdapter<H> {
    fn print(&mut self, c: char) { self.handler.print(c); }
    fn execute(&mut self, byte: u8) { self.handler.execute(byte); }
    // ... delegate all methods
}
```

## Supported Sequences

### CSI (Control Sequence Introducer)
| Sequence | Effect |
|----------|--------|
| CSI n A | Cursor up n |
| CSI n B | Cursor down n |
| CSI n C | Cursor forward n |
| CSI n D | Cursor back n |
| CSI n ; m H | Cursor position |
| CSI n J | Erase display |
| CSI n K | Erase line |
| CSI n m | SGR (colors/attrs) |

### OSC (Operating System Command)
| Sequence | Effect |
|----------|--------|
| OSC 0 ; title ST | Set window title |
| OSC 52 ; c ; data ST | Clipboard |

## File: crates/ftui-extras/src/terminal/parser.rs

## Unit Tests
- test_cursor_movement: All CSI cursor commands
- test_sgr_colors: 16, 256, and truecolor
- test_erase_commands: J and K variants
- test_utf8_graphemes: Multi-byte characters
- test_incomplete_sequence: Buffering partial escapes
- test_malformed_sequence: Graceful recovery

## Performance
- O(1) per byte processed
- No allocations in hot path (reuse buffers)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Deliver the behavior implied by the title: ANSI Parser Integration
- Alien Artifact: define invariants + failure modes; if heuristics exist, specify an explainable decision rule
- Extreme Optimization: baseline -> profile; include isomorphism proof for perf changes
- Unit tests: edge cases + regression fixtures; property tests for invariants where applicable
- E2E: deterministic mode + verbose JSONL logs (env, capabilities, timings, seed, checksums)

 {"id":"bd-2ueu.3","title":"Terminal State Machine","description":"Track terminal state from parsed sequences.

## Requirements
- Grid of cells with attributes
- Cursor position and visibility
- Scrollback buffer
- Terminal modes (wrap, origin, etc.)

## Proposed API

```rust
pub struct TerminalState {
    grid: Grid,
    cursor: Cursor,
    scrollback: Scrollback,
    modes: TerminalModes,
    dirty: DirtyRegion,
}

pub struct Grid {
    cells: Vec<Cell>,
    width: u16,
    height: u16,
}

#[derive(Clone, Copy)]
pub struct Cell {
    pub char: char,
    pub fg: Color,
    pub bg: Color,
    pub attrs: CellAttrs,
}

pub struct Cursor {
    pub x: u16,
    pub y: u16,
    pub visible: bool,
    pub shape: CursorShape,
    pub saved: Option<(u16, u16)>,  // For DECSC/DECRC
}

pub struct Scrollback {
    lines: VecDeque<Vec<Cell>>,
    max_lines: usize,
}

bitflags! {
    pub struct TerminalModes: u32 {
        const WRAP = 0b0001;
        const ORIGIN = 0b0010;
        const INSERT = 0b0100;
        const CURSOR_VISIBLE = 0b1000;
    }
}

impl TerminalState {
    pub fn new(width: u16, height: u16) -> Self;
    pub fn resize(&mut self, width: u16, height: u16);
    pub fn cell(&self, x: u16, y: u16) -> &Cell;
    pub fn cell_mut(&mut self, x: u16, y: u16) -> &mut Cell;
    pub fn move_cursor(&mut self, x: u16, y: u16);
    pub fn scroll_up(&mut self, lines: u16);
    pub fn scroll_down(&mut self, lines: u16);
    pub fn clear_region(&mut self, region: ClearRegion);
    pub fn dirty_region(&self) -> &DirtyRegion;
    pub fn mark_clean(&mut self);
}
```

## Dirty Tracking
Track which cells changed since last render. Options:
1. Bitmap (1 bit per cell) - memory efficient
2. Region list - good for block updates
Use bitmap for dense changes, region list for sparse.

## File: crates/ftui-extras/src/terminal/state.rs

## Unit Tests
- test_cursor_movement: Basic cursor ops
- test_scroll_up: Content shifts, scrollback fills
- test_scroll_down: Reverse scroll
- test_wrap_mode: Line wrap at edge
- test_resize: Content reflow
- test_dirty_tracking: Correct dirty detection

## Acceptance Criteria
- Deliver the behavior implied by the title: Terminal State Machine
- Alien Artifact: define invariants + failure modes; if heuristics exist, specify an explainable decision rule (evidence ledger / Bayes factors where applicable)
- Extreme Optimization: baseline -> profile -> opportunity matrix; for each perf change include an isomorphism proof + golden output checksums
- Unit tests: happy path + edge cases + regression fixtures; add property tests for core invariants where applicable
- E2E: scripts with verbose JSONL logging (env, capabilities, timings, seed, checksums) + deterministic mode

 {"id":"bd-2ueu.6","title":"Embedded Terminal E2E Test Suite","description":"## Purpose
End-to-end validation of embedded terminal widget functionality.

## Test Scenarios

### 1. PTY Management (term_pty_e2e.rs)
- Shell spawn success
- Environment inheritance
- Working directory
- Clean termination

### 2. ANSI Rendering (term_ansi_e2e.rs)
- 256 color output
- Cursor positioning
- Scrollback buffer
- Line wrapping

### 3. Input Forwarding (term_input_e2e.rs)
- Key to escape sequence
- Modifier combinations
- Paste bracketing
- Function keys

### 4. Real Programs (term_programs_e2e.rs)
- bash interactive session
- vim rendering correctness
- htop live updates
- less scrolling

### 5. Resize Handling (term_resize_e2e.rs)
- SIGWINCH propagation
- Content reflow
- Cursor preservation
- Rapid resize stability

## Logging Requirements
- INFO: PTY lifecycle events
- DEBUG: ANSI sequence parsing
- TRACE: Input byte sequences
- Metric: Render latency per frame

## Script Location
scripts/e2e/embedded_terminal/

## Known Program Tests
- bash, zsh shells
- vim, nano editors
- htop, top monitors
- less, more pagers

## Acceptance Criteria
- Deliver the behavior implied by the title: Embedded Terminal E2E Test Suite
- Alien Artifact: define invariants + failure modes; if heuristics exist, specify an explainable decision rule (evidence ledger / Bayes factors where applicable)
- Extreme Optimization: baseline -> profile -> opportunity matrix; for each perf change include an isomorphism proof + golden output checksums
- Unit tests: happy path + edge cases + regression fixtures; add property tests for core invariants where applicable
- E2E: scripts with verbose JSONL logging (env, capabilities, timings, seed, checksums) + deterministic mode

 {"id":"bd-2uk","title":"Implement Text type with styled spans","description":"# Implement Text type (Segment/Span collection)

## Background
Plan Section 0.4 and Appendix D establish a text pipeline:
- `Segment` is the atomic styled unit (Cow<str> where possible)
- higher-level markup/text APIs produce segments
- widgets/layout paint segments into `Frame`/`Buffer`

Text must support:
- ergonomic construction for app authors
- deterministic style application
- width-correct wrapping/truncation (ZWJ/emoji/combining)
- fast paths for ASCII-heavy workloads (see bd-v6y)

## Proposed model
A `Text` is an owned collection of segments (or spans) plus helpers.

Option A (generic, most faithful to plan):
```rust
pub struct Text<'a> {
    segments: Vec<Segment<'a>>,
}
```

Option B (ergonomic, no lifetimes in app code):
- `Text` stores `Segment<'static>` (Cow<'static, str>)
- constructors accept `&str` and own when needed

We should pick the option that keeps the public API pleasant while preserving the plan's "borrow when you can" benefit for string literals.

## Required behaviors
- Append/push spans/segments
- Apply a base style (inheritance) deterministically
- Split into lines (preserve styles)
- Truncate to width with ellipsis (width-correct)
- Wrap to width (width-correct)

## Width correctness
- ASCII fast path must be correct and allocation-free (bd-v6y)
- Non-ASCII path must use Unicode width + (ideally) grapheme segmentation
- Must not wrap mid-grapheme for common emoji/ZWJ sequences

## Tests
- Line splitting preserves styles across `\n`
- Truncation is width-correct for:
  - ASCII
  - CJK wide chars
  - emoji/ZWJ sequences
- Wrapping is width-correct and stable across widths

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `Text` type exists with an ergonomic builder API.
- [ ] Wrapping/truncation is width-correct.
- [ ] ASCII fast path is integrated (bd-v6y).
- [ ] Unit tests cover ASCII, CJK, emoji/ZWJ cases.

## Location
`ftui-text` crate","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-31T22:18:51.038800704Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-01T17:54:46.093864113Z","closed_at":"2026-02-01T17:54:46.093846229Z","close_reason":"Implemented Text type with Span, Line, and Text structs - ergonomic construction, base style application, truncation. 85 tests passing.  {"id":"bd-2v34","title":"Semantic Events: GestureRecognizer State Machine","description":"## Task

Implement the stateful `GestureRecognizer` that transforms raw events into semantic events.

## Implementation

Create `crates/ftui-core/src/gesture.rs`:

```rust
use crate::{Event, MouseButton, MouseEventKind, Position, SemanticEvent, ChordKey};
use std::time::{Duration, Instant};

/// Configuration for gesture recognition thresholds
#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
pub struct GestureConfig {
    /// Time window for double/triple click (default: 300ms)
    pub multi_click_timeout: Duration,
    /// Time before long press triggers (default: 500ms)  
    pub long_press_threshold: Duration,
    /// Minimum movement to start drag (default: 3 cells)
    pub drag_threshold: u16,
    /// Time window for chord completion (default: 1000ms)
    pub chord_timeout: Duration,
    /// Minimum velocity for swipe detection (default: 50 cells/sec)
    pub swipe_velocity_threshold: f32,
    /// Enable swipe gesture detection (default: false)
    pub enable_swipe: bool,
}

impl Default for GestureConfig {
    fn default() -> Self {
        Self {
            multi_click_timeout: Duration::from_millis(300),
            long_press_threshold: Duration::from_millis(500),
            drag_threshold: 3,
            chord_timeout: Duration::from_millis(1000),
            swipe_velocity_threshold: 50.0,
            enable_swipe: false,
        }
    }
}

/// Internal drag state tracking
#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
struct DragState {
    start_pos: Position,
    button: MouseButton,
    started: bool,  // Has DragStart been emitted?
}

/// Internal click tracking for multi-click detection
#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
struct ClickState {
    pos: Position,
    button: MouseButton,
    time: Instant,
    count: u8,
}

/// Stateful gesture recognizer
pub struct GestureRecognizer {
    config: GestureConfig,
    
    // Click tracking
    last_click: Option<ClickState>,
    
    // Drag tracking
    mouse_down: Option<(Position, MouseButton, Instant)>,
    drag_state: Option<DragState>,
    last_mouse_pos: Option<Position>,
    
    // Long press tracking
    long_press_pending: Option<(Position, Instant)>,
    
    // Chord tracking
    chord_buffer: Vec<ChordKey>,
    chord_start: Option<Instant>,
}

impl GestureRecognizer {
    pub fn new(config: GestureConfig) -> Self { /* ... */ }
    
    /// Process a raw event, returning any semantic events produced.
    /// May return 0, 1, or multiple semantic events.
    pub fn process(&mut self, event: &Event, now: Instant) -> smallvec::SmallVec<[SemanticEvent; 4]> {
        let mut results = smallvec::SmallVec::new();
        
        // Check for expired chords
        self.expire_chord(now);
        
        match event {
            Event::Mouse(mouse) => self.process_mouse(mouse, now, &mut results),
            Event::Key(key) => self.process_key(key, now, &mut results),
            _ => {}
        }
        
        results
    }
    
    /// Check if currently in a drag operation
    pub fn is_dragging(&self) -> bool {
        self.drag_state.as_ref().map(|d| d.started).unwrap_or(false)
    }
    
    /// Reset all gesture state (e.g., on focus loss)
    pub fn reset(&mut self) {
        self.last_click = None;
        self.drag_state = None;
        self.mouse_down = None;
        self.long_press_pending = None;
        self.chord_buffer.clear();
        self.chord_start = None;
    }
    
    /// Check for long press timeout (call from tick handler)
    pub fn check_long_press(&mut self, now: Instant) -> Option<SemanticEvent> { /* ... */ }
}
```

## Key Algorithms

### Double-Click Detection
1. On MouseUp, check if within timeout of last click
2. If same position (±1 cell tolerance) and same button → increment count
3. count=2 → DoubleClick, count=3 → TripleClick
4. Emit Click first, then multi-click if detected

### Drag Detection
1. On MouseDown, record position and time
2. On MouseMove, calculate distance from start
3. If distance > threshold → emit DragStart, then DragMove
4. On MouseUp during drag → emit DragEnd
5. On Escape during drag → emit DragCancel

### Chord Detection
1. On modifier+key press, start/extend chord buffer
2. If chord matches registered pattern → emit Chord
3. If timeout expires → clear buffer
4. Non-modifier key clears buffer

## Unit Tests

```rust
#[cfg(test)]
mod tests {
    use super::*;
    
    fn make_click(x: u16, y: u16, button: MouseButton) -> Event { /* helper */ }
    
    #[test]
    fn detects_double_click() {
        let mut recognizer = GestureRecognizer::new(GestureConfig::default());
        let now = Instant::now();
        
        // First click
        let events1 = recognizer.process(&make_click(5, 5, MouseButton::Left), now);
        assert!(events1.iter().any(|e| matches!(e, SemanticEvent::Click { .. })));
        
        // Second click within timeout
        let events2 = recognizer.process(&make_click(5, 5, MouseButton::Left), now + Duration::from_millis(100));
        assert!(events2.iter().any(|e| matches!(e, SemanticEvent::DoubleClick { .. })));
    }
    
    #[test]
    fn no_double_click_after_timeout() {
        let mut recognizer = GestureRecognizer::new(GestureConfig::default());
        let now = Instant::now();
        
        recognizer.process(&make_click(5, 5, MouseButton::Left), now);
        let events = recognizer.process(&make_click(5, 5, MouseButton::Left), now + Duration::from_millis(500));
        
        // Should be a new single click, not double
        assert!(!events.iter().any(|e| matches!(e, SemanticEvent::DoubleClick { .. })));
    }
    
    #[test]
    fn drag_starts_after_threshold() {
        let mut recognizer = GestureRecognizer::new(GestureConfig::default());
        let now = Instant::now();
        
        // Mouse down
        recognizer.process(&mouse_down(5, 5), now);
        
        // Small move - no drag yet
        let events1 = recognizer.process(&mouse_move(6, 5), now);
        assert!(!events1.iter().any(|e| matches!(e, SemanticEvent::DragStart { .. })));
        
        // Move beyond threshold
        let events2 = recognizer.process(&mouse_move(10, 5), now);
        assert!(events2.iter().any(|e| matches!(e, SemanticEvent::DragStart { .. })));
    }
    
    // ... more tests for all gesture types
}
```

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] GestureConfig with sensible defaults
- [ ] GestureRecognizer state machine
- [ ] Double/triple click detection
- [ ] Drag start/move/end detection
- [ ] Long press detection
- [ ] Chord sequence detection
- [ ] All unit tests passing
- [ ] No allocations in hot path (SmallVec)

## Alien Artifact Upgrades
- Model the core decision(s) with explicit state, priors/assumptions, and invariants; write down failure modes as first-class artifacts.
- Explainability: add an evidence ledger for any non-trivial decision (why this branch, why this fallback). Prefer Bayes factors / log-likelihood contributions over ad-hoc scoring.
- Guarantees: where prediction/thresholding exists, prefer distribution-free calibration (conformal prediction) or anytime-valid testing (e-process) so we can stop early without invalid inference.
- Graceful degradation: define capability tiers + deterministic fallbacks; never fail closed without a traceable reason.


## Extreme Optimization
- Baseline first: capture p50/p95/p99 for relevant command(s) with `hyperfine` (store raw output).
- Profile next: CPU flamegraph + allocation profile; identify top-5 hotspots.
- Opportunity matrix: implement only Score ≥ 2.0 (Impact×Confidence/Effort).
- Isomorphism proof per change: ordering, tie-breaking, RNG seeds, and golden checksums must match.
- Regression gates: add microbench + budgets; failures must print actionable deltas and include log artifacts.


## E2E Plan
- Provide/extend an E2E script that exercises the feature end-to-end in PTY (where applicable).
- Logging: verbose JSONL with env/capabilities + per-step timings + seed + checksums; include a summary at end.
- On failure: print reproduction command and point to artifacts directory.

 {"id":"bd-2vne","title":"Pi-style Keybinding UX & Input Semantics","description":"Define and implement a Pi-inspired keybinding model (Ctrl+C clears input, Esc cancels task, Esc Esc toggles tree) that avoids destructive conflicts in interactive UIs built on FrankenTUI.

## Success Criteria
- Epic delivers a cohesive user-visible capability: Pi-style Keybinding UX & Input Semantics
- Child tasks are not just implemented; each has unit tests + E2E scripts with verbose JSONL logs and deterministic fixtures
- Graceful degradation tiers are specified (capabilities, fallbacks, safety) and validated in tests


## Alien Artifact Upgrades
- Model the core decision(s) with explicit state, priors/assumptions, and invariants; write down failure modes as first-class artifacts.
- Explainability: add an evidence ledger for any non-trivial decision (why this branch, why this fallback). Prefer Bayes factors / log-likelihood contributions over ad-hoc scoring.
- Guarantees: where prediction/thresholding exists, prefer distribution-free calibration (conformal prediction) or anytime-valid testing (e-process) so we can stop early without invalid inference.
- Graceful degradation: define capability tiers + deterministic fallbacks; never fail closed without a traceable reason.


## Extreme Optimization
- Baseline first: capture p50/p95/p99 for relevant command(s) with `hyperfine` (store raw output).
- Profile next: CPU flamegraph + allocation profile; identify top-5 hotspots.
- Opportunity matrix: implement only Score ≥ 2.0 (Impact×Confidence/Effort).
- Isomorphism proof per change: ordering, tie-breaking, RNG seeds, and golden checksums must match.
- Regression gates: add microbench + budgets; failures must print actionable deltas and include log artifacts.


## Testing Plan
- Unit tests: happy path + edge cases + regression fixtures.
- Property tests: invariants (idempotence, monotonicity, determinism) where applicable.
- Fuzz tests: parsers/state machines and any input-facing logic.
- Determinism: fixed seeds/time-freeze; snapshot fixtures must be stable.
","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-02T22:13:00.862172653Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-02T23:13:37.140643134Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["harness","input","keybindings","runtime","ux"],"comments":[{"id":99,"issue_id":"bd-2vne","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Background
- User feedback from X praises Pi’s keybinding model: Ctrl+C clears prompt first; Esc cancels current task; Esc Esc opens a tree view. It avoids the common conflict where Ctrl+C both clears input and interrupts the agent.
- FrankenTUI is a kernel, but the harness and any agent-style app should model best‑in‑class interaction semantics. This epic targets a reference implementation that downstream apps can copy.

Goals
- Define a deterministic, documented keybinding policy that resolves the Ctrl+C vs interrupt ambiguity.
- Support multi-key sequences (Esc Esc) in a way that doesn’t regress normal Esc behavior.
- Ensure the model is testable and works in both inline and alt-screen modes.
- Keep behavior optional and configurable so embedding apps can opt out.

Non‑Goals
- Do not mandate global keybindings for all FrankenTUI apps; provide reference behavior + tooling, not a hard requirement.
- Do not change core terminal handling in ways that break existing examples without an explicit migration.

Risks / Considerations
- Esc is overloaded in terminals; detecting a double-Esc without blocking single-Esc requires a small timing window and careful event buffering.
- Ctrl+C should be treated as “clear input” only when input is non-empty; otherwise it should fall through to “interrupt / quit” behavior (configurable).
- Inline mode + log streaming must not reorder output when toggling views (tree overlay). Ensure the one-writer rule is preserved.

Deliverables
- A written keybinding policy and state machine.
- A sequence/chord interpreter (either in runtime utility or harness-local) with tests.
- A harness view showing the tree toggle and task cancellation semantics.
- Documentation so future selves understand intent and rationale.

 {"id":"bd-2vne.1","title":"Keybinding policy spec (Ctrl+C vs Esc vs Esc Esc)","description":"Write a self-contained spec for input clearing vs task cancel, including timing for Esc sequences, conflict/compatibility matrix, and default fallthroughs.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Deliver the behavior implied by the title: Keybinding policy spec (Ctrl+C vs Esc vs Esc Esc)
- Alien Artifact: define invariants + failure modes; if heuristics exist, specify an explainable decision rule (evidence ledger / Bayes factors where applicable)
- Extreme Optimization: baseline -> profile -> opportunity matrix; for each perf change include an isomorphism proof + golden output checksums
- Unit tests: happy path + edge cases + regression fixtures; add property tests for core invariants where applicable
- E2E: scripts with verbose JSONL logging (env, capabilities, timings, seed, checksums) + deterministic mode
","acceptance_criteria":"## Acceptance Criteria
- Policy defines Ctrl+C / Esc / Esc Esc precedence, with terminal-specific exceptions.
- Includes unit-test plan for keybinding resolution and conflict detection.
- Includes E2E test plan that simulates key sequences and logs resolved action, focus owner, and latency.
- Logging schema includes key sequence, action outcome, and state snapshot IDs.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-02T22:13:16.090703791Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-03T03:49:28.513844796Z","closed_at":"2026-02-03T03:49:28.513819047Z","close_reason":"Spec complete: docs/spec/keybinding-policy.md covers all acceptance criteria - priority table, terminal conflict matrix, unit test plan, E2E test plan, and JSONL logging schema","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["design","keybindings","ux"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2vne.1","depends_on_id":"bd-2vne","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-02T22:13:16.090703791Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":100,"issue_id":"bd-2vne.1","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Scope
- Document the exact behavior for: Ctrl+C, Esc, Esc Esc, and any fallback (Ctrl+D, Ctrl+Q).
- Provide a state machine for input vs task-running states.

Key Decisions to Nail Down
- Ctrl+C behavior when input is non-empty: should clear input and keep task running.
- Ctrl+C behavior when input is empty: should cancel active task; if no task, quit (configurable).
- Esc behavior: should cancel current modal/overlay; if none, cancel task or clear input depending on state.
- Esc Esc: reserved for “tree view” toggle. Define timing window and interaction with single Esc.

Implementation Notes
- Use a short debounce window (e.g., 200–350ms) for Esc Esc detection; if second Esc doesn’t arrive in time, treat as single Esc.
- Keep sequence detection isolated to app logic or a small runtime utility to avoid entangling low-level input parsing.

Acceptance Criteria
- Spec includes: state diagram, priority order, and timing window rationale.
- Spec lists configurables and default values.
","created_at":"2026-02-02T22:13:23Z"},{"id":147,"issue_id":"bd-2vne.1","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Additions
- Include an explicit conflict matrix for terminals where Esc is ambiguous, and the chosen default behavior.
- Document accessibility considerations (e.g., single-hand use, muscle memory from shells/CLI tools).
","created_at":"2026-02-02T22:22:24Z"}]}
 {"id":"bd-2vne.2","title":"Implement key sequence/chord interpreter","description":"Add a small interpreter for multi-key sequences (e.g., Esc Esc) with timeout handling, independent of low-level input parsing.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Deliver the behavior implied by the title: Implement key sequence/chord interpreter
- Alien Artifact: define invariants + failure modes; if heuristics exist, specify an explainable decision rule (evidence ledger / Bayes factors where applicable)
- Extreme Optimization: baseline -> profile -> opportunity matrix; for each perf change include an isomorphism proof + golden output checksums
- Unit tests: happy path + edge cases + regression fixtures; add property tests for core invariants where applicable
- E2E: scripts with verbose JSONL logging (env, capabilities, timings, seed, checksums) + deterministic mode
","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-02T22:13:27.714259503Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-03T03:56:27.645959667Z","closed_at":"2026-02-03T03:56:27.645939569Z","close_reason":"Implemented key_sequence.rs with KeySequenceInterpreter providing feed(event, now) -> Action API with Emit(KeyEvent), EmitSequence { kind, keys }, and Pending variants. 21 tests pass, clippy passes.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["input","keybindings","runtime"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2vne.2","depends_on_id":"bd-2vne","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-02T22:13:27.714259503Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-2vne.2","depends_on_id":"bd-2vne.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-02T22:16:24.612403544Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":101,"issue_id":"bd-2vne.2","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Design Notes
- Keep this higher-level than raw input parsing. It should operate on KeyEvent stream from `ftui-core`.
- Provide a small API: `feed(event, now) -> Action` where Action can be `Emit(event)`, `EmitSequence(seq)`, or `Pending`.
- Use a single timer window for Esc sequences to avoid adding complexity.

Edge Cases
- If Esc is part of a terminal escape sequence, it should already have been parsed into a KeyEvent; do not re-interpret those raw bytes here.
- Avoid blocking: if pending a sequence, allow the rest of the runtime loop to continue (tick/poll).

Acceptance Criteria
- Timeout window is configurable.
- Unit tests cover single Esc, double Esc within window, and timeout fallback.
","created_at":"2026-02-02T22:13:33Z"}]}

 {"id":"bd-2vne.6","title":"Keybinding action mapping + state machine helper","description":"Implement a reusable action-mapping layer that interprets KeyEvent stream into high-level actions based on input/task state and policy spec.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Deliver the behavior implied by the title: Keybinding action mapping + state machine helper
- Alien Artifact: define invariants + failure modes; if heuristics exist, specify an explainable decision rule (evidence ledger / Bayes factors where applicable)
- Extreme Optimization: baseline -> profile -> opportunity matrix; for each perf change include an isomorphism proof + golden output checksums
- Unit tests: happy path + edge cases + regression fixtures; add property tests for core invariants where applicable
- E2E: scripts with verbose JSONL logging (env, capabilities, timings, seed, checksums) + deterministic mode

- Separate “what key was pressed” from “what action should the app take,” using the policy spec.
- Keep the logic reusable so harness and future apps can share it.

Behavioral Scope
- Inputs: KeyEvent stream + state flags (input_nonempty, task_running, modal_open, view_overlay).
- Output: High-level actions like ClearInput, CancelTask, ToggleTree, Quit, PassThrough.
- Includes explicit priority order (e.g., modal cancel before task cancel).

Acceptance Criteria
- Action mapping matches policy spec and is unit-tested.
- Minimal API surface, no tight coupling to harness UI structures.
